---
title: |
  <span style="font-size: 1.0em;">Survival Trees and Random Survival Forests</span>
subtitle: |
  <div style="font-size: 0.8em; line-height: 1.6; text-align: center;">
  Jakob Haas <br>
  **Supervisor:** Dr. Andreas Bender <br>
  </div>
date: "2026-06-05"
format:
  revealjs:
    css: customstyle.css
    html-math-method: mathjax
    title-slide-attributes: 
      data-background-image: ./results/Titelfolie.png
      data-visibility: hidden
    slide-number: c
    center: false
editor: source
bibliography: references.bib
filters: 
  - pseudocode
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cran.rstudio.com"))
source("setup.R")
```

## {.visibility-hidden}

![](results/Titelfolie.png)

## Introduction

- We have discussed various classical statistical approaches for handling time-to-event or survival data

::: {.fragment}

![](results/ml_pipeline.png){length=90%}

:::

::: {.fragment}
- We now want to adapt the machine learning workflow to survival data
::: 



## Outline

1.  From CART to Survival Tree

2.  From Survival Tree to Random Survival Forest

3.  Evaluation

4.  Benchmark

5.  Conclusion



## CART: Construction

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width = "45%"}
![](results/iris_tree.png){width=100%}
:::

::: {.column width = "55%"}

**How CART works**

1.  Start with a root node that contains all the data

2.  Search for the feature $x_p$ and split that minimizes the empirical risk in the child nodes

3.  Repeat recursively for each node

4.  Stop when a stopping criterion is reached. The nodes where the algorithm stops are called terminal nodes.


:::

::::

## CART: Prediction



:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width = "45%"}
![](results/iris_tree.png){width=100%}
:::


::: {.column width = "55%"}
**New Observation:**
```{r}
new.observation <- data.frame(
  Feature = c("Sepal Length", "Sepal Width", "Petal Length", "Petal Width"),
  Value = c(5.1, 3.5, 2.8, 0.2)
)

new.observation|>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    Feature = "Feature",
    Value = "Observed Value"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = Value,
    decimals = 1
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = px(18),
    column_labels.font.weight = "bold",
    table.width = pct(90),
    data_row.padding = px(5)
  )
```

::: {.fragment}

$\Rightarrow$ Hard-Label Prediction: $\widehat{Y} = \text{versicolor}$   
$\Rightarrow$ Probabilities: 
$$
\widehat{P}[\text{species} = \text{setosa}] = 0.00
$$
$$
\widehat{P}[\text{species} = \text{versicolor}] = 0.91
$$
$$
\widehat{P}[\text{species} = \text{virginica}] = 0.09
$$
::: 
:::
::::




## From CART to Survival Tree

- Now we want to apply this idea to survival data
- Recap: What is survival data?
$$
(T_i, \delta_i, \mathbf{x}_i)
$$
where
- $T_i$ is the observed time for individual $i$
- $\mathbf{x}_i$ is the corresponding covariate vector
- $\delta_i$ is the event indicator:
$$
\delta_i =
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if the event is observed,} \\
0, & \text{if the observation is censored.}
\end{cases}
$$

## From CART to Survival Tree

- A first natural approach would be to fit a regression tree with the observed times $T_i$ as target variable
- Why does this not work?
- For survival data, the response is not fully observed due to censoring
- The regession tree would handle the response as if the event occured at      $T_i$ which is not the case for censoring
- Image we have for example: 
    - $(T_1,\delta_1)=(100,1), \qquad (T_2,\delta_2)=(100,0)$ 
    
    - Then we have $T_1 = T_2 = 100$, but $T_1^\ast = 100,\; T_2^\ast > 100$.   

$\Rightarrow$ Survival trees therefore require censoring-aware split criteria




## Survival Trees: Log-rank Splitting

- A split at node $h$ on a feature $x$ is always of the form $x \leq c$ and $x > c$
- So as a result we get two new sets of data (child nodes).
- A good split maximizes the differences of survival probabilities ($S(t)$) between the two sets
- Notation:
    - $t_1 < t_2 < ... < t_N$: distinct event times in parent node $h$
    - $d_{j, g}$: number of events at time $j$ in child node $g \in \{1, 2\}$
    - $d_j = d_{j ,1} + d_{j, 2}$
    - $n_{j, g}$: number at risk immediately before $t_j$ in child node $g \in \{1, 2\}$
    - $n_j = n_{j, 1} + n_{j, 2}$

    
## Survival Trees: Log-rank Splitting
- Remember the log-rank statistic:
$$
L(x, c) = \frac{\sum_{j =1}^{N} \left(d_{j, 1} - n_{j ,1} \frac{d_j}{n_j}\right)}{\sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \frac{n_{j, 1}}{n_j} \left(1 - \frac{n_{j, 1}}{n_j}\right)
\left(\frac{n_j - d_j}{n_j - 1}\right) d_j}}
=
\frac{\sum_{j = 1}^{N} (d_{j, 1} - E[d_{j, 1}])}{\sqrt{Var[d_{j, 1}]}}
$$

:::{.fragment}
- High values of $|L(x, c)|$ provide evidene againt $H_0$: Both groups have equal survival functions
- Hence, our goal is to maximize $|L(x, c)|$ $\Rightarrow$ we want to have a huge survival differnce between the resulting child nodes
- Formally find $x^\ast$ and $c^\ast$ such that
$$
|L(x^\ast, c^\ast)| \geq |L(x, c)| \quad \forall x, c
$$ 
:::


## Example: Log-rank splitting

- We take the **tumor** data from the $\{\textit{pammtools}\}$ package
- We consider the two binary features **complications** $\in \{0, 1\}$ and **transfusion** $\in \{0, 1\}$

::: {.fragment}
![](results/logrank_splits_tumor.png){width=100%, length=100%}
:::

:::{.fragment}
- Which split will we choose
:::








    






## Survival Trees: Prediction Targets
- Instead of predicting $t \mid \mathbf x$ we want to predict survival properties
    1. Survival function with the Kaplan-Meier estimator [@kaplan1958nonparametric]
$$
\widehat{S}_h(t) = \prod_{j: t_{j, h} \leq t} \left(1 - \frac{d_{j, h}}{n_{j, h}}\right)
$$
    2. Cummulative Hazard function with the Nelson-Aalen estimator:
$$
\widehat{H}_h(t) = \sum_{j: t_{j, h} \leq t} \frac{d_{j, h}}{n_{j, h}}
$$

- We assume that all observations in the terminal nodes have the same $\widehat{S}_h(t)/\widehat{H}_h(t)$

## Example: Survival Tree
- We consider the **tumor** data with features, **age**, **sex**, and **transfusion**



## From Survival Trees to Random Survival Forests

- A single survival tree is interpretable, but often unstable:
  small changes in the training data can lead to substantially different tree structures
- This instability results in high variance and may reduce predictive performance

::: {.fragment}
- **Random Survival Forests** [@ishwaran2008random] address this issue by combining many randomized survival trees
- Randomization is introduced through:
  - bootstrap sampling of the training data for each tree
  - random selection of candidate features at each split $mtry$)

$$
\text{Random Survival Forest} =
\text{Bagging}
+
\text{Random Feature Selection}
+
\text{Survival Trees}
$$
:::


## Algorithm
- Inputs:
    - $\mathbb{D}_{\text{train}}$: Training-data with target $y$ and $P$ features
    - $B:$ Number of Trees
    - $mtry \leq P:$ Number of features that are considered at each split
    - $d_0 \geq 0:$ Minimum number events in each node
    
::: {.fragment}
1. Draw $B$ bootstrap samples from the data. Each sample contains around $67 \%$ of the data. The other $33\%$ are called out-of-bag-data (OOB-data)
2. Grow a survival tree with each of the $B$ samples. At each split consider $mtry$ random features for your split. Grow the tree under the condition that each node must have $d_0 \geq 0$ events
3. Calculate the Cumulative Hazard Function (CHF) for each tree. Then average over all $B$ CHFs to obtain the ensemble prediction

:::


## Aggregation of Survival Predictions
Now we want to make predictions with our tree ensemble:    

- Imagine we have a new observation $\widetilde{x}$  
- We push $\widetilde{x}$ down the $B$ trees    
- For every tree we obtain a prediction for the Cumulative Hazard Function $H_{b}(t \mid  \widetilde{x})$
- Then we average over all trees to obtain our ensemble prediction:
$$
\widehat{H} (t \mid \widetilde{x}) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b = 1}^{B} H_{b}(t \mid  \widetilde{x})
$$
- To obtain the Survival function we can use the Breslow estimator:
$$
\widehat{S}(t \mid \widetilde{x}) = \exp(- \widehat{H} (t \mid \widetilde{x}))
$$

## OOB-data
- Remember in each bootstrap sample we only used $67\%$ of the data to grow the survival tree
- The remaining $33\%$ are the out-of-bag-data (OOB-data)






## Variable Importance

- Random Survival Forests provide a prediction-based measure of variable
  importance based on OOB observations.
- For a variable $x_p$, the Variable Importance (VIMP) is calculated as follows
  [@ishwaran2008random]:

    1. Obtain the original OOB prediction error of the forest.
    2. Pass each OOB observation down its corresponding OOB trees.
       Whenever a split on $x_p$ is encountered, randomly assign the
       observation to one of the two daughter nodes.
    3. Compute the prediction error of this perturbed OOB ensemble.
    4. Define

$$
    \operatorname{VIMP}(x_p)
    =
    \operatorname{Err}_{\mathrm{perturbed}}(x_p)
    -
    \operatorname{Err}_{\mathrm{OOB}}.
$$



## Variable Importance

- Example: Consider the **tumor** data again


![](results/feature_importance_rsf.png)

- How can we interpret that?

## Evaluation: Concordance Index

- Harrell's C-index measures the discriminatory ability of a survival model
  [@harrell1982evaluating]
- It evaluates whether observations with earlier observed events have higher predicted risk

::: {.fragment}
**Comparable Pairs**    
For two observations $i$ and $j$ with $T_i$ < $T_j$, the pair is comparable if $\delta_i = 1$  
- The earlier observation is an observed event  
- If observation $i$ is censored before $T_j$, their event ordering is unknown    
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Which pairs are comparable?**
![](results/graph_c_index.png)

:::

## Concordance Index: Intuition
![](results/c_index_plot.svg)

- The C-index evaluates ranking, not the absolute accuracy of predicted survival probabilities


## Concordance Index: Calculation

$$
\widehat{C}
=
\frac{
|\text{concordant pairs}|
+
0.5 \cdot | \text{risk ties} |
}{
|\text{comparable pairs} |
}.
$$

::: {.fragment}
$$
\widehat{C} = 1
\quad \Rightarrow \quad
\text{perfect risk ranking}
$$
$$
\widehat{C} = 0.5
\quad \Rightarrow \quad
\text{ranking comparable to chance}
$$

:::

::: {.fragment}

**For Random Survival Forests:**  
- Each patient receives a scalar risk score $\widehat r_i$  
- A possible RSF risk score is the ensemble mortality derived from the
  predicted cumulative hazard function  
$$
\widehat r_i \uparrow
\quad \Rightarrow \quad
\text{higher predicted event risk}
$$

:::


## Brier-Score
- The Brier-Score is a measure for probabilistic outcomes
- Let $\pi(t) \in [0, 1]$ the predicted probability at t and $c(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ the true value at t. Then the Brier-Score at time $t$ is given by:
$$
BS(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i = 1}^{N} [\pi_i(t) - c_i(t)]^2
$$

::: {.fragment}
- This ideas was extended by  @graf1999assessment, such that the Brier-Score can serve as a measure for survival problems with censored informations
- For calculation we refer to @wang2019machine

:::

## Brier-Score Calculation
- We reweight the individual contributions to the empirical Brier-Score to take censoring information into account
- The, we can compute the Score as follows:
$$
BS(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i(t) [\widehat{S}_i(t) - Y_i(t)]^2,
$$
where $Y_i(t) = I [T_i^\ast > t]$ and
$$
w_i(t) = \begin{cases}
\frac{\delta_i}{G(T_i)} & \text{ if} \quad T_i \leq t \\
\frac{1}{G(T_i)} & \text{ if} \quad T_i > t
\end{cases}
$$
- G is the Kaplan-Meier estimator of the censoring distribution for a dataset $(T_i, 1 - \delta_i, \mathbf x_i)$


## Benchmark: Setup

::: {.benchmark-tables}

- We evaluate survival predictions on two tasks:

| Task | Data set |
|---|---|---|
| `Survival::gbsg` | Breast cancer patients |
| `Pammtools::tumor` | Tumor registry patients |

::: {.fragment}

- We compare four learners:

| Learner | Role |
| --- | --- |
| Kaplan-Meier | Non-inforamtive baseline without covariates |
| Cox regression | Classical statistical semi-parametric approach |
| Random Survival Forest | Untuned ML method |
| Tuned Random Survival Forest | ML method with optimized hyperparameters |
:::

:::

## Benchmark: Setup
- The tuned RSF is optimized by random search over:
$$
\texttt{mtry.ratio} \in [0.1,1],
\qquad
\texttt{nodesize} \in \{3,\ldots,50\}.
$$

- Inner resampling: 3-fold cross-validation
- Outer evaluation: repeated 5-fold cross-validation with 3 repetitions
- Evaluation metrics:
  - C-index
  - Integrated Brier Score
  
## Benchmark: Results C-Index
![](results/benchmark_cindex.png){width = 100%}

## Benchmark: Integrated Brier Score
![](results/benchmark_graf.png){width = 100%}





## Conclusion



## Sources
